text: Modern surgery meets an old procedure, laparoscopic surgery for peptic ulcer disease. We present a forty-four year old female with a six year history of peptic ulcer disease. She conveys a history of chronic PPI treatment, smoking, and severe reflux symptoms. A recent EGD revealed esophagitis and a hiatal hernia. Pyloric stenosis that corresponds to your symptoms. She underwent multiple endoscopic dilations. Twenty-four-hour pH monitoring and manometry is also performed. This reveals acid reflux with a normal lower esophageal sphincter.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic truncal vagotomy, hiatal hernia repair, and subtotal gastrectomy with Bilroth II reconstruction. The lesser omentum below the hiatus is divided using a ligature, revealing the right corner of the diaphragm. The peritoneum anterior to the esophagus is divided and extended laterally, exposing the superior aspect of the left crura. The right crura is further exposed, and the posterior vagus nerve is identified and transected with a ligature. A segment of the posterior vagus nerve is sent for frozen section to pathology, confirming the presence of nerve fibers.

The surgeon identifies the anterior left vagus nerve and excises a segment using ligature. The excised segment is sent to pathology for frozen section, confirming the presence of anterior vagus nerve fibers. The surgical team searches for additional branches but finds none.

An NG tube is visible in the esophagus, which is retracted to the left to free it posteriorly, exposing the left crura of the diaphragm. The mobilized esophagus is moved intra-abdominally, and the hiatus is tightened with a 2-0 silk suture, visibly narrowing the opening to prevent further herniation.

The surgeon performs a subtotal gastrectomy by dividing the gastrocolic omentum near the greater curvature of the stomach, carefully preserving the gastroepiploic arcade. The dissection progresses from the lower antrum upwards to the hemigastrectomy point, continuing along the greater curvature to the first portion of the duodenum, just before the gastroduodenal artery.

The surgeon divides the lesser omentum close to the gastric wall, extending the division just above the first branch of the crow's foot. The remaining lesser omentum is further divided beyond the pylorus into the first portion of the duodenum. The first portion of the duodenum beyond the pylorus is transected using an endogia echelon-sixty with blue staples, and the transected duodenum and pylorus are retracted superiorly.

The surgeon clears and divides adhesions from the posterior aspect of the stomach. The stomach is then transected between two cleared areas using an endo-GIA echelon stapling device with green staples and peristrips, completing the division with three applications. The nasogastric tube is retracted to the gastroesophageal junction before the stomach is divided.

The surgeon displaces the omentum and identifies the transverse colon, lifting it to reveal the ligament of Treitz. The proximal jejunum is then brought in an anticolic fashion to the remaining gastric pouch.

Two stay sutures are placed in a serosal muscular fashion between the jejunum and the posterior wall of the remaining gastric pouch, ensuring no tension is applied. The proximal jejunum is positioned towards the greater curvature, while the distal part is oriented towards the lesser curvature.

The surgeon creates an opening in the posterior gastric wall and the anterior jejunal antimesenteric wall using electrocautery. The Endo-GIA Echelon-60 stapler with a blue cartridge is introduced through the opening into the jejunum and stomach, then advanced, closed, and fired for fifteen seconds. The staple line is inspected, confirming no bleeding.

The surgeon closes the opening in the stomach and jejunum using continuous threo-PDS sutures, ensuring all layers are included. A second serosomuscular interrupted layer with threoethabond is added to reinforce the closure. The afferent loop of the gastrojejunostomy is fixed to the greater curvature of the stomach with an additional suture to prevent kinking, and a similar fixture is applied to the descending limb. An air leak test is performed to confirm the airtightness of the anastomosis.
An upper GI study in post-op day one is performed, showing the absence of a leak. The patient was discharged on post-op day three on a post-gastrectomy diet.
